front: Work: a signet ring found in the belly of a fish breaks a curse, allowing King Dushyanta to remember his wife
Work: the sage Durvasa curses a king to forget his wife until he sees a signet ring
Work: a king meets the title character at a hermitage after nearly killing a deer
Work: the prologue features a Stage Director instructing an actress and a 'Benediction Upon the Audience'
Work: Goethe based the 'Prelude' of Faust on the prologue of this play
Work: a seven-act play in Sanskrit based on a story from the Mahabharata
Work: the play ends with the title character's son found playing with lion cubs
Work: Dushyanta recalls telling a woman to 'Count every day one letter of my name' after giving her a ring
Work: an ascetic prophesies that a king will father a universal emperor after sparing a black hermitage antelope
Work: a fisherman tells policemen including Suchaka and Januka about finding a ring in a carp
Work: the title nymph is turned into a vine after entering a forbidden forest
Work: the title character writes of her love on a birch leaf, which is seen by her lover's wife
Work: the title character is banished from heaven when she accidentally says her real lover's name during a play-within-a-play
Work: a king has a 'mad scene' talking to a peacock, elephant, and river while searching for his lover
Work: the protagonist is rescued from the demon Keshin by her lover
Work: King Agnimitra falls in love with a dancing servant who turns out to be a princess
Work: a jester tricks the queen into giving up her ring to free an imprisoned dancer, using a faked snake bite
Work: in the prologue, a stage assistant expresses surprise at performing a new author's work instead of older playwrights
Work: an exiled yaksha sends a cloud to carry a message to his wife in the city of Alaka
Work: a 111-stanza poem written in the seventeen-syllable Mandakranta ('slow-stepper') meter
Work: the exiled speaker grows too thin for his bracelets during a year-long exile
Work: the title figure passes the 'dread shrine' of Shiva in Ujjain on its way to Alaka
Work: an epic poem that begins with a description of the Himalayas as a 'measuring-rod' of the world
Work: in the fourth canto, the widow of a character burned to death mistakes his outline in the ashes for him still alive
Work: this author's earliest work, divided into six cantos about different seasons, focused on erotic love
Author: legendarily took up writing after a princess rejected him for being an unintelligent shepherd
Author: Mohan Rakesh's 1958 play One Day in the Season of Rain depicts a love triangle involving this Sanskrit author
Author: Goethe wrote an epigraph about this author: 'Wouldst thou the earth and heaven itself in one sole name combine?'
